reasoning:
The threshold for this question is approximately 1,358 fatalities over a 30-day period (which requires an average of ~45 deaths per day). 
Since the ceasefire took effect on October 8, 2025, casualty rates have dropped dramatically. As of early 2026, the Gaza Ministry of Health and OCHA report roughly 420-450 deaths over the roughly 90 days of the ceasefire, or ~140-150 per month (less than 5 per day). 
For the first resolution date (Jan 25, 2026), the 30-day window covers mostly the past month where only ~150 deaths occurred, meaning there is practically zero mathematical chance of reaching 1,358 in the remaining week (they would need over 1,000 deaths). 
For the second resolution date (Feb 17, 2026), reaching 1,358 would require a complete collapse of the ceasefire and an immediate return to large-scale warfare producing >45 deaths daily for a full month. With the US declaring "Phase Two" of the ceasefire on Jan 18, 2026, the trend strongly points toward continued de-escalation, making a massive spike in deaths highly improbable. I assign a very low probability for the second date, marginally higher than the first to account for the tail risk of a ceasefire collapse.
